1: The second lot (gôrāl šēnî) comes out for Simeon. The ordinal 'second' implies the lots were drawn in sequence, each new lot determining who comes next. Simeon's lot falls bəṯôk naḥălat bənê-yəhûḏāh — 'within the inheritance of the children of Judah.' The preposition bəṯôk (H8432, in the midst of, inside) signals immediately that this allotment is a sub-grant within a prior one — anomalous from the start.
2: Simeon's cities begin with Beersheba (bəʾēr šebaʿ H884 — Well of Seven or Well of the Oath), the southernmost city of Israel in the formulaic 'from Dan to Beersheba' (Judges 20:1). The patriarchal associations of Beersheba are dense: Abraham's covenant with Abimelech (Genesis 21:31), Isaac's altar (Genesis 26:23-25), Jacob's departure for Egypt (Genesis 46:1). Simeon inherits the southernmost sacred geography.
3: Hazar-shual (H2705, Fox-enclosure), Balah, and Azem — the city list for Simeon continues with mostly Negeb towns. The place names often encode ecological reality: ḥāṣēr (H2691, enclosure/settlement) appears in many compound names, describing the settled villages of the Negeb where semi-nomadic clans established permanent sites.
4: Eltolad, Bethul, Hormah — Hormah (H2767, H2763 root: ḥāram, devoted to destruction) marks a site that Israel vowed to destroy (Numbers 21:3). The name preserves the memory of a ḥērem (sacred destruction) vow. When Simeon inherits this city, the vow has already been fulfilled — the name now is a memorial of obedience rather than an ongoing commitment.
5: Ziklag, Beth-marcaboth, Hazar-susah — Ziklag (H6860) will later be given to David by the Philistine king Achish (1 Samuel 27:6) — at that point a city not yet fully under Israelite control. Its inclusion in Simeon's list reflects the theoretical allotment; actual possession would be established later.
6: Beth-lebaoth and Sharuhen — thirteen cities with their villages (ḥăṣērêhem H2691). The summary formula 'cities and their villages' distinguishes between urban centers and their dependent rural settlements — the ancient equivalent of a city and its suburbs or satellite hamlets.
7: Ain, Rimmon, Ether, Ashan — four more cities. Ain (H5871, spring) and Rimmon (H7417, pomegranate) are sometimes treated as a compound Ain-rimmon (cf. Nehemiah 11:29). The fruit-bearing name Rimmon points to the agricultural richness of the Negeb's settlements when wells were maintained and terraces built.
8: All the villages around these cities to Baalath-beer (Ramah of the Negeb) — the summary includes unnamed villages around the named cities. ḥăṣērîm (villages, H2691) are the agricultural satellites; ḥāṣēr originally described an unwalledenclosure around which temporary structures clustered. Simeon's inheritance is defined as much by this dispersed rural network as by its named cities.
9: The explanation of Simeon-within-Judah: kî-hāyāh ḥēleq bənê-yəhûḏāh rāḇ mēhem — 'because the share of Judah was too great for them' (lit. too much from them). ḥēleq (H2506, portion/share) is here used of the land itself. The surplus of Judah is redistributed to the landless Simeonites — an economic solution that simultaneously fulfills Jacob's curse on Simeon (Genesis 49:7: 'I will scatter them in Israel'). The lot mechanism has achieved what prophecy announced.
10: The third lot comes up for Zebulun. wayyaʿal gôrāl šəlîšî — 'the third lot came up.' The sequence of lot-drawing continues; each lot determines the next tribe to be placed. Zebulun's territory is in the lower Galilee — Genesis 49:13 promised that 'Zebulun will live by the seashore' and 'his haven will be Sidon.' The geographic fulfillment of the patriarchal blessing is a subtext of these allotment chapters.
11: Zebulun's boundary: wayyaʿal gəḇûlām goes up toward the sea, toward Maralah, touching Dabbesheth. The boundary description uses the same 'going up/down' topographic vocabulary as Benjamin's (Joshua 18), tracking the actual relief of the Galilee hill country. Zebulun's territory straddles the Jezreel Valley and lower Galilee.
12: The boundary turns eastward from Sarid toward Beth-shemesh. Zebulun's eastern boundary runs toward the Jordan Valley — the tribe sits between the Mediterranean coastal plain and the Sea of Galilee, controlling the important trade routes through the Jezreel Valley (the Via Maris corridor).
13: From there it passes eastward to Gath-hepher (H1662) and Eth-kazin, and continues to Rimmon, curving to Neah. Gath-hepher is the hometown of the prophet Jonah (2 Kings 14:25) — its inclusion in the tribal list demonstrates how the inheritance chapters contain the geographic seeds of later prophetic narratives.
14: The boundary turns northward to Hannathon and ends at the Valley of Iphtah-el. The circuit of Zebulun's territory is complete: a compact tribal region in the lower Galilee, well-watered and agriculturally rich — the same region where Jesus will conduct the majority of his Galilean ministry.
15: Kattath, Nahalal, Shimron, Idalah, Bethlehem — twelve cities (a different Bethlehem from Judah's, in Zebulun's territory). The recurrence of the name Bethlehem (H1035, house of bread) in multiple tribal territories reflects how common names derived from agricultural productivity (leḥem, bread) were reused across regions.
16: This is the inheritance (naḥălāh) of Zebulun with its cities and villages. The closing formula for each tribe's allotment standardizes the record: inheritance (covenant term), cities (urban centers), villages (rural network). The formula's consistency implies a common source document — likely a royal cadastral record.
17: The fourth lot comes out for Issachar. Genesis 49:14-15 described Issachar as 'a rawboned donkey crouching between its saddlebags' — a tribe comfortable with agricultural labor and content with its territory. The Jezreel Valley, which Issachar inherits, is the most fertile land in Canaan.
18: Issachar's cities: Jezreel (yizrəʿēʾl H3157, 'God sows/plants') — the valley itself takes its name from this city. Chesulloth, Shunem — Shunem (H7766) will be the home of the great woman who hosts Elisha (2 Kings 4:8), demonstrating again how the allotment lists embed the geographic home of later narratives.
19: Hapharaim, Shion, Anaharath — further Issacharite cities in the Jezreel Valley corridor. The valley these cities occupy connects the Mediterranean coast to the Jordan Valley, making Issachar's territory a major crossroads of ancient trade and military movement.
20: Rabbith, Kishion, Abez — Kishion (H7191) is in the territory that becomes the battle site of Deborah and Barak (Judg 4-5, the Kishon River area). The geographic precision of the allotment lists creates the stage on which subsequent narrative drama unfolds.
21: Remeth, En-gannim, En-haddah, Beth-pazzez — four cities whose names encode landscape features: ʿêyn (spring), gannîm (gardens), ḥaddāh (sharp/keen, possibly describing a distinctive spring). The spring-names are the most ancient layer of geographic memory, preserved because water sources were permanent landmarks.
22: The territory touches Tabor, Shahazumah, and Beth-shemesh — sixteen cities in total for Issachar. Mount Tabor (taḇôr H8396) is the prominent isolated peak at the northeastern corner of the Jezreel Valley — a major geographic landmark that will serve as the assembly point for Deborah's coalition (Judges 4:6). Its inclusion as a boundary marker acknowledges its unmistakable visual dominance.
23: This is the inheritance of Issachar — closing formula. Issachar's territory in the Jezreel Valley gives it the most agriculturally productive land in Canaan, fulfilling Jacob's portrait of a tribe content with its fertile portion (Genesis 49:15).
24: The fifth lot comes out for Asher. Genesis 49:20: 'Asher's food will be rich; he will provide delicacies fit for a king.' Asher's coastal territory along the Phoenician border (Carmel to Sidon) gave it access to seafaring trade and the fertile Acco plain. The lot confirms what the patriarchal blessing anticipated.
25: Asher's cities: Helkath, Hali, Beten, Achshaph — the northern coastal plain. Achshaph (H407) appears in Joshua 11:1 among the northern coalition against Israel; its inclusion in Asher's allotment confirms the conquest. The transformation from enemy city to inheritance city marks the completion of each locality's history.
26: Alammelech, Amad, Mishal — and it reaches Carmel to the sea and to Shihor-libnath. The explicit boundary 'to the sea' (hayyāmmāh) fulfills the coastal character of Asher's territory. Mount Carmel (H3760) is the great promontory jutting into the Mediterranean — the border point between Asher and Manasseh. Elijah's contest with Baal's prophets will occur here (1 Kgs 18).
27: The boundary turns eastward to Beth-dagon, touches Zebulun and the Valley of Iphtah-el, northward to Beth-emek and Neiel, then continues to Cabul. Bêt-dāgôn (H1016, house of Dagon) — a place dedicated to the Philistine grain/fish deity. The city's pagan name is recorded without comment; Asher inherits the city as real estate, its prior cultic associations now subsumed under covenant ownership.
28: Ebron, Rehob, Hammon, Kanah — as far as Great Sidon. The northern boundary reaching to Sidon (ṣîḏōn H6721) fulfills the prophetic scope of Asher's territory (Genesis 49:13 gives the seacoast to Zebulun, but Asher's northern reach toward Sidon is clearly anticipated). The tribe settles on the boundary of Phoenician civilization — a strategic and culturally complex position.
29: The boundary turns to Ramah and the fortified city of Tyre (ṣōr H6865), then turns toward Hosah and ends at the sea. Tyre (the Rock) is the great Phoenician maritime city — its inclusion as a boundary marker signals that Asher's northern limit was never effectively occupied. The gap between allotment and actual control characterizes these northern territories throughout the period of the Judges.
30: Ummah, Aphek, Rehob — twenty-two cities. Aphek (H663, fortress) appears multiple times in biblical narrative — the site of Philistine encampment before the ark's capture (1 Samuel 4:1) and of other strategic engagements. The catalog of Asher's cities records both what was granted and what was never fully possessed.
31: This is the inheritance of Asher — closing formula. Asher's coastal territory and trade connections made it potentially the wealthiest tribe; its proximity to Phoenicia brought cultural pressure as well as commercial opportunity. The failure to drive out the Sidonians (Judges 1:31) will become a chronic spiritual vulnerability.
32: The sixth lot comes out for Naphtali. Genesis 49:21: 'Naphtali is a doe set free that bears beautiful fawns' — a portrait of agility and eloquence. Naphtali's territory in the Upper Galilee and around the Sea of Chinnereth (Galilee) is mountainous and fertile, suited to a free-ranging tribe.
33: Naphtali's boundary from Heleph, the oak in Zaanannim, Adami-nekeb, and Jabneel to Lakkum, ending at the Jordan. The oak in Zaanannim (ʾēlôn bəṣaʿanannîm H436 H6815) will later appear as a landmark in Judges 4:11 where Heber the Kenite pitches his tent — the specific tree mentioned in the allotment list is the same tree under which the later narrative unfolds.
34: The boundary turns westward to Aznoth-tabor, continuing to Hukkok, touching Zebulun on the south and Asher on the west, and Judah at the Jordan on the east. The phrase 'Judah at the Jordan' is geographically puzzling since Judah does not reach the Jordan in the north — possibly referring to a Danite enclave or a textual variant. The three-directional tribal contact (Zebulun, Asher, Judah) describes Naphtali's landlocked position in the north.
35: The fortified cities of Naphtali: Ziddim, Zer, Hammath, Rakkath, Chinnereth — Chinnereth (H3672) gives its name to the Sea of Chinnereth (= Sea of Galilee). The city sits on the northwestern shore of the lake. Hammath (H2575, hot springs) preserves the memory of natural thermal springs at the site of later Tiberias.
36: Adamah, Ramah, Hazor — Hazor (H2674) is the great city-state conquered in Joshua 11, whose king Jabin is a recurring antagonist. Its inclusion in Naphtali's territory is significant: the tribe inherits the most powerful Canaanite capital, yet Judges 4 shows a Jabin of Hazor still oppressing Israel — the allotment records what was granted; the narrative records the ongoing struggle to hold it.
37: Kedesh, Edrei, En-hazor — Kedesh (H6943, sanctuary/holy place) in Naphtali will become one of the six cities of refuge designated in Joshua 20. The name (qōḏeš, holy, H6944) encodes its function as a sacred asylum site. Its designation as a refuge city is consistent with its ancient character as a sanctuary place.
38: Iron, Migdal-el, Horem, Beth-anath, Beth-shemesh — nineteen cities. Beth-anath (H1043, house of Anath) and Beth-shemesh (H1053, house of the sun) carry the names of Canaanite deities — Anath (warrior goddess) and Shemesh (sun god). Naphtali inherits cities named for the gods its neighbors worshiped, which may explain the spiritual pressure Naphtali faces throughout Judges.
39: This is the inheritance of Naphtali — closing formula. Naphtali's upper Galilean territory, surrounding the Sea of Galilee, is the same region Isaiah identifies as the land of darkness that will see a great light (Isaiah 9:1-2), fulfilled in Jesus's Galilean ministry (Matthew 4:12-16). The allotment of Naphtali plants the geographic seed of the gospel's opening movement.
40: The seventh lot comes out for Dan. Dan is the last tribe to receive its allotment through the lot; its position last in the sequence may reflect its later migration story (see v47). Genesis 49:16-17: 'Dan will judge his people as one of the tribes of Israel. Dan will be a serpent by the roadside' — an ambiguous portrait of cunning and independence.
41: Dan's territory: Zorah, Eshtaol, Ir-shemesh (city of the sun, another sun-deity name). Zorah and Eshtaol will be the hometown of Samson (Judges 13:2,25) — the great Danite judge. The allotment list situates the Samson narrative in its precise geographic home.
42: Shaalabbin, Aijalon (ʾAyyālôn H357, deer or gazelle place), Ithlah — Aijalon is the valley where Joshua commanded the sun and moon to stand still (Joshua 10:12-13). The city of Aijalon (ʿêmeq-ʾayyālôn) gives its name to that event. It now falls in Dan's allotment, embedding the memory of the miraculous battle in the tribe's geographic inheritance.
43: Elon, Timnah, Ekron — Timnah (H8553) will be where Samson finds his Philistine wife (Judges 14:1), and where his father-in-law later gives her to another man (Judges 14:20). Ekron (H6138) is one of the five Philistine cities — listed in Dan's allotment though never captured. The gap between allotment and possession is starkest for Dan.
44: Eltekeh, Gibbethon, Baalath — Gibbethon (H1405) appears later as a Philistine city (1 Kings 15:27; Joshua 16:15-17). Baalath (H1191, lady/Baal-place) indicates a pre-Israelite Baalistic cult site; Dan inherits territory saturated with Canaanite religious sites, which helps explain the tribe's eventual adoption of idolatrous worship (Judges 18:30-31).
45: Jehud, Bene-berak, Gath-rimmon — Bene-berak (H1139, sons of lightning) is later a city in the territory of Benjamin/Dan whose inhabitants were known to the Assyrian king Sennacherib (noted in his annals). The inheritance of cities later mentioned in extrabiblical documents confirms the geographic reality behind the allotment lists.
46: Me-jarkon and Rakkon, with the border opposite Joppa (yāpôʾ H3305). Joppa (modern Jaffa) is the main Mediterranean port of the central coast — the gateway through which Jonah will flee (Jonah 1:3) and from which cedar for Solomon's temple will be transported (2 Chronicles 2:16). Dan's territory touches the coast at this strategic port, though the tribe never effectively controlled it.
47: wayyēṣēʾ gəḇûl-bənê-ḏān mēhem wayyaʿălû bənê-ḏān wayilaḥămû ʿim-lešem — 'when the territory of Dan slipped from their grasp, the Danites went up and fought against Leshem.' The verb yāṣāʾ mēhem (H3318 + H4480 prep.) means the border 'went out from them' — the territory they were allotted proved impossible to hold. The Philistines' military pressure in the coastal plain made Dan's original inheritance untenable. This verse is the honest acknowledgment that the ideal allotment did not match the realized possession. lešem = Laish (cf. Judges 18:27) — Dan's migration to the far north is recorded here in compressed form; Judges 18 expands the story in detail.
48: The Danites captured Leshem, put it to the sword, took possession, and called it Dan after their ancestor. wayyiqrəʾû ʾōtāh dān — the city is renamed for the tribe. The renaming of captured territory is a covenant act: the tribe claims the city as its own by inscribing its name on it. The migration means Dan's actual possession ends up far north of its original allotment, near the headwaters of the Jordan.
49: wayəkallû liḥlōq ʾet-hāʾāreṣ ligbûlōtêhā — 'when they had finished distributing the land according to its borders.' The verb kālāh (H3615, to complete, finish) marks the conclusion of the distribution process. ligbûlōtêhā (according to its borders) confirms the boundary-based system. The Israelites then give Joshua his inheritance — the commander who oversaw everyone else's distribution receives his last.
50: At the LORD's command (ʿal-pî YHWH) they give Joshua Timnath-serah in the hill country of Ephraim, which he requested. ʾašer šāʾal — 'which he asked/requested.' Joshua requests his own city; it is not assigned to him through the lot. ʿal-pî YHWH (by the mouth of YHWH) means the grant was divinely authorized. wayyiḇneh ʾet-hāʿîr — 'he built the city' — a founding act; Joshua does not merely inherit an existing city but establishes one, consistent with the portrait of a leader who creates rather than merely occupies.
51: ʾēlleh hannəḥālōt ʾašer niḥălû ʾelʿāzār hakkōhēn wîhôšuaʿ bin-nûn wərāʾšê hāʾāḇôt lamaṭṭôt bənê-yiśrāʾēl bəgôrāl bəšilōh lipnê YHWH — the comprehensive closing formula names the three authorities: Eleazar the priest, Joshua son of Nun, and the heads of the ancestral tribes. bəgôrāl (by lot) and lipnê YHWH (before YHWH) confirm both the mechanism and the theological locus. waytikallênāh ḥalleqet hāʾāreṣ — 'and the apportioning of the land was complete' (kālāh again: finished). The distribution is closed.
